content:
# Ruth Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 882 | **Source Image:** RUT_004_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Meanwhile, Boaz went up to the town gate and sat down there. Before long, the very redeemer he had mentioned came walking by. Boaz called out, "Come over here, friend — sit down." So the man came and sat down.

**2** Then Boaz gathered ten of the town's elders and said, "Sit down here." They sat down.

**3** He said to the redeemer, "Naomi, who has come back from Moab, is selling the piece of land that belonged to our relative Elimelech.

**4** I thought I should bring this to your attention and say: Buy it in the presence of these seated here — in the presence of the elders of my people. If you will redeem it, redeem it. But if you will not, tell me, so I will know — because there is no one besides you to redeem it, and I am next after you." The man said, "I will redeem it."

**5** Then Boaz said, "On the day you acquire the land from Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead man, in order to raise up the name of the dead on his inheritance."

**6** At that, the redeemer said, "Then I cannot redeem it — it would jeopardize my own inheritance. Take my right of redemption for yourself, because I am not able to redeem it."

**7** Now in former times in Israel, to finalize any transaction involving redemption or exchange, one man would remove his sandal and hand it to the other. This was how agreements were publicly confirmed in Israel.

**8** So the redeemer said to Boaz, "Buy it yourself," and he pulled off his sandal.

**9** Then Boaz announced to the elders and all the people, "You are witnesses today that I am buying from Naomi everything that belonged to Elimelech, and everything that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon.

**10** I am also acquiring Ruth the Moabite, Mahlon's widow, as my wife, in order to raise up the name of the dead man on his inheritance, so that his name will not disappear from among his relatives or from the records of his town. You are witnesses today."

**11** All the people at the gate and the elders said, "We are witnesses! May the LORD make the woman entering your house like Rachel and like Leah, the two who together built up the house of Israel. May you prosper in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem.

**12** And through the offspring the LORD gives you from this young woman, may your house become like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah."

**13** So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. He slept with her, and the LORD enabled her to conceive, and she gave birth to a son.

**14** The women of the town said to Naomi, "Blessed be the LORD, who has not left you without a redeemer today! May this child become famous throughout Israel!

**15** He will renew your life and sustain you in your old age, for your daughter-in-law who loves you has given birth to him — she who is worth more to you than seven sons."

**16** Naomi took the child, cradled him in her arms, and became his nurse.

**17** The neighborhood women gave him a name, saying, "A son has been born to Naomi!" They named him Obed. He became the father of Jesse, who was the father of David.

**18** This is the family line of Perez: Perez fathered Hezron,

**19** Hezron fathered Ram, Ram fathered Amminadab,

**20** Amminadab fathered Nahshon, Nahshon fathered Salmon,

**21** Salmon fathered Boaz, Boaz fathered Obed,

**22** Obed fathered Jesse, and Jesse fathered David.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "friend":** The Hebrew uses the enigmatic פְּלֹנִי אַלְמֹנִי (peloni almoni), roughly "so-and-so." The narrator pointedly withholds this man's name — the one who refuses redemption is forgotten, while the one who embraces it (Boaz) is remembered for all time. "Friend" captures the casual address while preserving the anonymity.

**Verse 5 — The pivotal condition:** Boaz strategically reveals that acquiring the land also means marrying Ruth and raising children in Mahlon's name. The unnamed redeemer's willingness evaporates — he would invest in land whose inheritance would pass to another man's line. Boaz understood human nature and played his hand masterfully.

**Verse 7 — The sandal custom:** The narrator pauses to explain this practice to readers for whom it was already archaic, suggesting the book was composed well after the events it describes. The sandal symbolized walking rights — authority over land.

**Verse 11 — Rachel and Leah:** The community blesses Ruth by comparing her to the matriarchs who built Israel. This is extraordinary — a Moabite woman is being welcomed into the heart of Israel's story. The mention of Perez and Tamar (v. 12) recalls another boundary-crossing woman whose bold action continued the line of Judah (Genesis 38).

**Verse 15 — "worth more to you than seven sons":** In a culture where sons were the ultimate measure of blessing, this is the highest possible praise. Seven symbolizes completeness. Ruth, a foreign woman, is declared to exceed the value of a perfect number of sons.

**Verse 17 — "A son has been born to Naomi":** The women's declaration completes the reversal of chapter 1. Naomi, who returned "empty," now holds fullness in her arms. The God who seemed to have turned against her has been quietly working through the loyal love of a Moabite daughter-in-law.

**Verses 18–22 — The genealogy:** The story that began with famine, death, and emptiness ends with the royal line of David. Ruth the Moabite — outsider, widow, gleaner — stands in the direct ancestry of Israel's greatest king. This genealogy is the final theological statement: God's redemptive purposes transcend every boundary humans construct.

— PNEUMA
